findings.title: PR description's premise about 'two grade.py files' is incorrect; only humanise/grade.py exists in the diff
findings.suggested_fix: No code change. The reviewer's prompt asked us to inspect 'humanise/grade.py vs dev/evals/grade.py' but git ls-files shows only humanise/grade.py is tracked. Importers in run_skill_creator_iteration.py, run_grade_sweep.py and dev/evals/test_grade.py all load humanise/grade.py via importlib — single source of truth, no drift.
findings.why_it_matters: Suspected duplication does not exist. The grader logic is centralised; risk of divergence is low.
findings.evidence: Diff at lines 4056 onwards modifies humanise/grade.py only. `ls dev/evals/grade.py` returns no such file. test_grade.py at line 2315 explicitly loads ROOT/humanise/grade.py.

findings.title: run_claude crashes on null token-count fields rather than degrading to 0
findings.suggested_fix: Coerce each usage field via `int(usage.get('input_tokens') or 0)` instead of `int(usage.get('input_tokens', 0))`. With explicit nulls in usage JSON, the current expression evaluates `None + 0` and raises TypeError, which escapes the surrounding `except json.JSONDecodeError` and is caught only by the worker-level `except Exception`, marking the whole eval run as ERROR purely because of token accounting.
findings.why_it_matters: A single API quirk would silently invalidate eval runs, costing a re-run for every affected job. Anchor 75: the path from null usage field to TypeError is mechanical; whether the API actually emits nulls vs omits keys is the unknown.
findings.evidence: Lines 165-174 sum usage.get(...) values via `int(usage.get("input_tokens", 0) + ...)`. The default 0 is only used when the key is absent; if present-and-null, addition fails. The list-branch above (line 158-167) uses `int(entry.get("input_tokens", 0))` per-call which has the same vulnerability.

findings.title: Successful eval runs are graded twice (once inline in as_completed, once in grade_completed_runs)
findings.suggested_fix: Remove either the inline `grade_run(item, run_dir)` call inside the as_completed loop (line 986/996) or the trailing `grade_completed_runs(evals)` (line 999). Both write the same grading.json with the same content, doubling I/O and grader CPU per iteration. Since the inline grade_run is needed for failed-job paths to write a useful grading.json, keep that branch and drop grade_completed_runs.
findings.why_it_matters: Wasted CPU and a confusing code path that suggests one of the two sites was meant to be a fallback but neither is conditional. Anchor 100: trace the loop — every successful future calls grade_run; every job appears in evals so grade_completed_runs covers all the same dirs.
findings.evidence: Lines 984-997 call grade_run inside `for future in concurrent.futures.as_completed(future_map)`. Line 999 then calls `grade_completed_runs(evals)` which iterates evals and calls grade_run again where `outputs/response.md` exists.

findings.title: catalogue_hits' third terms.add line is a no-op, weakening the matcher silently
findings.suggested_fix: `terms.add(label.lower().replace("ai", "AI").lower())` lowercases the result of replacing 'ai' with 'AI', producing the same string as label.lower(). The intent looks like adding a case variant (e.g., 'AI' uppercase), but the trailing .lower() cancels it. Either drop the line or compute a real variant such as `label` (preserving original casing) or label.lower().replace(" ai ", " ").strip() to add a no-AI-prefix variant.
findings.why_it_matters: Anchor 100: trivially provable by reading the chain. The matcher still works (the other two terms catch most cases) but the function quietly does less than the author seemingly intended, which can swing audit-flags-present pass/fail at the boundary.
findings.evidence: Line 293: `terms.add(label.lower().replace("ai", "AI").lower())`. Both .lower()s produce identical strings since label.lower() already has only lowercase letters; replacing 'ai' with 'AI' then lowercasing returns to label.lower().

findings.title: extract_generated_text falls back to entire output when no Rewrite/Draft header found, causing rewrite-passes-* graders to score the audit text
findings.suggested_fix: When neither `**Rewrite**` nor `**Draft**` header is present in a rewrite/write eval (id 4-7), return an empty string rather than the full output. The grader then fairly scores 'rewrite missing' rather than scoring the audit prose (which legitimately quotes AI tells from the input) and producing artificial failures. Optionally surface a separate 'rewrite_block_missing' failure so the eval distinguishes 'rewrite is bad' from 'rewrite was never produced'.
findings.why_it_matters: Trace: an LLM that returns audit-only output for a rewrite eval will be re-graded over a body that contains quoted em-dashes and quoted AI vocabulary. regrade(...).fails will be high purely because of the audit's own quoted evidence, which is structurally guaranteed to contain the patterns. The eval falsely reports 'rewrite failed grading' when the truth is 'rewrite never produced'. Anchor 75: the path is mechanical for any output missing those headers.
findings.evidence: Line 273 (`extract_generated_text`): `return section_text(output, "Rewrite") or section_text(output, "Draft") or output`. section_text returns '' when header missing; '' is falsy, so the chain falls through to `output`. Then line 348-355's regrade runs on `generated == output`.

findings.title: run_codex temp file leaks on subprocess.TimeoutExpired
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the subprocess.run call in a try/finally that always unlinks output_path. Currently if subprocess.run raises TimeoutExpired (or the worker thread is interrupted), the tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) created at line 184 is never cleaned. Each timed-out codex run leaves a humanise-codex-output-*.md file in /tmp.
findings.why_it_matters: Slow-burn /tmp pollution across iteration runs. Not a logic bug but a resource leak the worker error-handler doesn't cover. Anchor 75: the leak path is mechanical; the only question is how often timeouts fire in practice.
findings.evidence: Line 184-185 creates output_path with delete=False. Line 201-207 calls subprocess.run with timeout=900. Lines 208-216 cleanup is inside `try/finally` but the try begins AFTER subprocess.run completes successfully — TimeoutExpired raised at line 201 propagates before reaching the cleanup.

findings.title: humanise/examples ai.txt: 100KB unreferenced text file with a space in its filename shipped inside the skill directory
findings.suggested_fix: Move to dev/evals/manual-pastes/ (where similar working artifacts live) or delete. Filenames with spaces break shell tab-completion and require quoting in scripts; shipping 100KB of unreferenced AI-generated samples inside the skill directory bloats the deliverable users will install.
findings.why_it_matters: The skill directory is the artifact users install. Including a 100KB unreferenced sample file confuses what the skill ships. Not a correctness bug for code paths, but a clear intent-vs-implementation mismatch: it's a working artifact in a published location.
findings.evidence: diff line 3473-3478 adds humanise/examples ai.txt as a new file. grep across humanise/ and dev/evals/ for 'examples ai' returns no references. The file's content is raw prompt-and-response text, not a skill input.

findings.title: regrade(text='') would still execute every check on empty string; rewrite-passes-* could give misleading 'pass' on truly-empty rewrite blocks
findings.suggested_fix: regrade('', depth='balanced') runs all 43 checks on an empty string. Several pattern detectors return passed=True for empty input (no patterns to find), so an empty rewrite would score `fails=0` and pass `rewrite-passes-balanced-grader`. Combined with the rewrite-produced assertion gating on word count >= 50 (grade_one_assertion line 334), this is mostly defended — but if the LLM emits exactly the **Rewrite** header with whitespace and no body, generated.strip() is '' and the empty-text-passes-grader would still fire. Add a guard `if not text.strip(): return {...fails: max_fails}` or document that callers MUST verify rewrite-produced before trusting rewrite-passes-*.
findings.why_it_matters: Anchor 50: depends on whether the LLM ever emits an empty rewrite block, which is observable but not seen in committed iterations. Defensive change.
findings.evidence: regrade at line 2679 doesn't guard against empty text. ALL_CHECKS detectors generally treat empty input as passing. `rewrite-produced` requires gen_words >= max(50, ...) but is a separate assertion; passing rewrite-passes-* alone doesn't imply rewrite-produced passed.

findings.title: build_prompt embeds full input file contents into prompt; large samples may push past --add-dir-mediated context limits or trip prompt-length validation
findings.suggested_fix: build_prompt reads file_blocks as `path.read_text()` and embeds the full markdown into the prompt. For long-form essays around 8-15KB this is fine; for the largest sample (e.g. 21c-image-first-men-original-sins.md which can run several thousand words) the prompt grows large but well under model context. No fix unless evals start including books. Note as residual risk only.
findings.why_it_matters: Anchor 25: speculative — depends on future corpus expansion. The skill prompt also instructs Read access (--allowedTools 'Read,Bash'), so the file is also reachable by tool — embedding may be redundant.
findings.evidence: Lines 90-95: file_blocks include the full file text. Line 109 builds the prompt string. The cmd at line 119-130 also passes --add-dir str(ROOT), so the agent could read files via tools instead.

findings.title: dev/evals/evals.json.bak (pre-existing) is committed; flagged because PR scope mentions it
findings.suggested_fix: Backup files should not live in version control; rely on git history instead. `git mv evals.json.bak` to a /dev/research/snapshots/ location if the historical state is genuinely useful, or `git rm` and let `git show <commit>:dev/evals/evals.json` retrieve the prior version on demand.
findings.why_it_matters: Documentation hygiene; not a correctness bug. PR review prompt explicitly invited a single finding noting its presence.
findings.evidence: ls -la dev/evals/evals.json.bak shows 24KB file dated 28 Apr 17:55, predating PR commits. Not in this PR's diff but visible in the working tree.

residual_risks: analyze_sample_body uses `text.split('---', 2)` to skip front-matter. If a sample has a horizontal-rule `---` near the top without YAML/JSON front-matter, the splitter would misidentify the body. Current corpus has well-formed front-matter so this is latent.

catalogue_hits assertion-counting is substring-based against catalogue labels. Mentions of words like 'em dashes' or 'curly quotes' inside the audit's prose (even when stating the *absence* of a pattern) increment the hit count. With audit templates that say 'no curly quotes detected', this could inflate audit-flags-present counts on clean drafts. Mitigated only by the LLM's chosen wording, not by parsing.

run_skill_creator_iteration.py uses ThreadPoolExecutor with default worker count 8 for Claude/Codex subprocesses. Each worker spawns a long-lived (up-to-900s) subprocess. With 18 evals × 2 configs (in include-old-skill mode), 36 jobs at 8 workers can saturate API rate limits or local CPU; no backoff/retry logic exists. A single throttled subprocess marks the eval as ERROR and continues without retry.

extract_audit_reported_count uses `re.match` with the exact prefix 'Referenced (\d+) catalogue pattern'. If the evidence string format ever changes (e.g., adds quotes around the number, or shifts to 'Catalogue patterns referenced:'), the regex silently returns None and audit-fidelity computation reports 'n/a'. Tightly couples the parser to the formatter.
testing_gaps: No test exercises run_skill_creator_iteration.py end-to-end with a stub Claude/Codex executor. The 1079-LOC orchestration is only validated via real LLM calls into iteration-* directories, which are checked in as outputs but cannot regression-test the orchestration logic itself.

grade_one_assertion has 23+ branches (one per assertion name). No unit test enumerates the assertion-name → handler dispatch; an assertion misnamed in evals.json would surface only at run time as 'KeyError(name)' wrapped into 'grader error for X'. A 60-second test that loads evals.json and calls grade_one_assertion with stub texts would catch typos before a 30-minute eval run.

extract_generated_text + section_text logic for splitting Rewrite/Draft sections has no unit test. The fallback-to-full-output behaviour (see finding 5) is the most likely bug surface and is currently untested.
regrade with empty string is untested. Combined with the rewrite-produced word-count gate, this is probably defended in practice but a 3-line test would lock the contract.

analyze_sample_body's front-matter heuristic has no test. If a future sample uses a different front-matter shape (e.g., HTML comment, +++ delimiters, or no front-matter with a horizontal rule near the top), body stats will silently shift without alerting.
